summary:
The Battle of Salamis (480 BC) was the decisive naval engagement of the [[event:1001|Greco-Persian Wars]], fought in the strait between the island of Salamis and the Attic mainland. The Greek fleet under Athenian commander Themistocles lured the larger Persian fleet into the narrow strait where superior numbers became a liability, crushing Persian naval power and forcing Xerxes to withdraw much of his army.

After the fall of Athens to Persian forces — the city was evacuated on Themistocles's urging, its citizens carried to safety by sea — the question of where to make a stand divided the Greek alliance. Peloponnesian commanders wanted to fight in waters near Corinth; Themistocles insisted on the Salamis strait, where the constricted space would negate Persian numerical advantage. Legend holds that he sent a secret message to Xerxes urging him to attack at night before the Greeks could flee, manipulating the Persians into the engagement on Greek terms.

The battle was fought in the cramped strait on a September morning. The heavier, more maneuverable Greek triremes rammed and disabled the Persian vessels crowded in the narrows. Persian crews who reached shore were killed by Greek infantry; Xerxes watched the disaster from a throne set on the hills above. By afternoon, the Persian fleet had lost roughly two hundred ships and effective control of the Aegean. Xerxes withdrew to Persia with much of his army, leaving a land force under Mardonius to winter in Thessaly.

The following spring, Mardonius's army was destroyed at the [[event:1006|Battle of Plataea]], ending the Persian invasion permanently. Salamis was the turning point that made this outcome possible. Themistocles's strategic brilliance — persuading the Athenians to build a fleet rather than distribute the silver windfall from Laurion individually — proved the decisive choice of the entire war.
